summary: Gruber responds to Lukas Mathis's counterargument about Nintendo's handheld gaming strategy, acknowledging Nintendo's history of succeeding with inferior hardware but arguing the post-iPhone landscape is fundamentally different. The key shift is that buying decisions moved from 'or' (choosing between handhelds) to 'and' (buying a handheld in addition to the smartphone you already carry). He draws parallels to point-and-shoot cameras, music players, and BlackBerry — all dedicated devices losing to smartphones despite technical superiority in their niche. Gruber argues Nintendo must bring its games to iOS or face irrelevance, as kids are growing up reaching for iPads over dedicated gaming devices.
key_points: The post-iPhone buying decision shifted from 'or' (choosing between handhelds) to 'and' (carrying an extra device alongside your smartphone), which is an entirely new competitive dynamic for Nintendo
Point-and-shoot camera sales declined 42% in early 2013, illustrating the same cliff facing dedicated handheld gaming devices
People fundamentally do not want to carry extra devices, and this single behavioral truth is what threatens every dedicated gadget category
Nintendo's best-case scenario with its current strategy is maintaining an ever-shrinking niche selling to nostalgic adults, not reaching new generations of kids
Sony's pivot to making camera sensors for smartphones represents the pragmatic 'if you can't beat 'em, join 'em' approach Nintendo should consider
money_quotes: What's different about the post-iPhone world of mobile computing is that the buying decision is no longer about or, it's about and.
People do not want to carry extra devices. It's that simple.
Post-PC mobile devices are eating the world.
The best they can do with their current strategy — the best — is maintain a position in an ever-shrinking niche, selling devices to nostalgic adults.
A Nintendo that doesn't make games for iOS is a Nintendo that doesn't reach today's kids; a Nintendo that doesn't reach today's kids is a Nintendo with no future.
It's a choice for Nintendo between playing the actual hand they've been dealt, crummy though it may be, or playing the hand they wish they held.
To quote my six year old daughter, barely looking up from her iPad: 'What's a Nintendo?'
key_insight: The smartphone didn't beat dedicated handhelds by being better at games — it beat them by already being in everyone's pocket, transforming the buying decision from choosing between devices to justifying carrying an extra one.
